title: Long-Term Dividend Investing
tagline: Become an independent dividend-growth investor in ~10 months.
description: A first-principles path from complete beginner to independent analyst. You learn to read businesses, not tickers: understand money and markets, read financial statements, master dividend safety and growth, value a company sensibly, manage risk, build a portfolio, and finish by analysing real stocks on your own. Built for the long-term owner, not the trader.
outcome: Produce a complete, decision-grade investment report on three real dividend stocks and a personal investing framework you keep for life.

stages.title: Money, Markets & the Investor Mindset
stages.summary: Build the temperament of an owner and understand what stocks, markets, and dividends fundamentally are.
objectives: Understand compounding, inflation, and total return over decades.
Know what a share, an index, and a dividend actually represent.
Tell investing apart from speculating and trading.
mistakes: Confusing a high yield with high quality — a 9% yield usually signals an expected cut, not free money.
Believing investing is about predicting prices rather than owning good businesses.
Underestimating compounding because early growth looks boring; the magic is in the final decade.
Treating a price drop as a loss when, for a long-term net buyer, it is an opportunity.

tasks.title: Read “The Psychology of Money”
tasks.summary: Set your temperament before touching any numbers.
tasks.whatYouGain: A calm, long-horizon temperament — the biggest predictor of investing success, and the mental armour to hold through fear.
details: Read it as a human, not an analyst. It frames behaviour, patience, and the role of luck and time.
Take note of the chapters on compounding and on ‘enough’ — they will anchor your decisions for decades.

resources.label: The Psychology of Money — Morgan Housel

tasks.title: Read “The Little Book of Common Sense Investing”
tasks.summary: Understand why most people fail, and the index you are trying to beat.
tasks.whatYouGain: Clarity on why most active investors lose to a simple index, so every stock you later pick must clear that bar.
details: Bogle's lessons on costs, churn, and emotion are the guardrails for any stock-picker.
Even as a future stock-picker, you must understand low-cost indexing as your benchmark.

resources.label: The Little Book of Common Sense Investing — John C. Bogle

tasks.title: Read The Intelligent Investor, chapters 8 & 20
tasks.summary: The two chapters that are the philosophical bedrock: Mr. Market and margin of safety.
tasks.whatYouGain: Two mental models you'll use forever: treating the market as a moody business partner, and never buying without a margin of safety.
details: Chapter 8 — The Investor and Market Fluctuations: the 'Mr. Market' metaphor.
Chapter 20 — Margin of Safety: the single most important idea in investing.
Skip the rest for now; you will return to the full book in Stage 5.

resources.label: The Intelligent Investor — Benjamin Graham

tasks.title: Build a compounding spreadsheet
tasks.summary: Model $10,000 at 7% for 30 vs 40 years and feel the difference.
tasks.whatYouGain: A visceral, first-hand feel for how compounding explodes in the final decade — proof that time in the market beats timing it.
details: Columns: year, contribution, return rate, dividends reinvested, balance.
Compare a 30-year vs 40-year horizon to see how the last decade dominates.
Run the Rule of 72 by hand for 4%, 7%, and 10% returns (years to double ≈ 72 ÷ return%).

tasks.title: Open a brokerage account & explore a quote page
tasks.summary: Find a real stock's price, market cap, dividend yield, and ex-dividend date.
tasks.whatYouGain: Fluency with a real brokerage and quote page, so terms like yield, market cap, and ex-dividend date stop being abstract.
details: Don't fund it heavily yet — this is reconnaissance.
Locate and write down: price, market cap, dividend yield, payout frequency, ex-dividend date.

tasks.title: Milestone: write your Investing Policy Statement
tasks.summary: A one-page statement of goals, horizon, contributions, and your crash-reaction plan.
tasks.whatYouGain: Your own written constitution — a rulebook that keeps you rational when markets are not.
details: Include: goals, time horizon, target monthly contribution, your behaviour plan for a 30% drawdown, and a one-sentence philosophy.
You will revise this document at the end of every stage. It is your North Star and your defence against future panic.

stages.title: Understanding Businesses & Moats
stages.summary: Learn to read a business model and the competitive advantage that makes a dividend durable.
objectives: Describe how any company makes money in plain language.
Identify moat type and width: brands, switching costs, network effects, cost advantages, scale.
Judge pricing power and capital intensity.
caseStudies: Coca-Cola — brand moat, pricing power, and unmatched global distribution made a simple product a great compounder.
Procter & Gamble — a portfolio of trusted everyday brands producing steady cash that funds 65+ years of raises.
mistakes: Buying products you love instead of businesses that make money.
Assuming today's moat is permanent — moats erode (Kodak had a wide moat, and film).
Ignoring capital intensity: two firms with equal profits can have very different free cash flow.
Straying outside your circle of competence because a story sounds exciting.

tasks.title: Read “The Little Book That Builds Wealth”
tasks.summary: The clearest book ever written on economic moats.
tasks.whatYouGain: A reusable lens for spotting durable competitive advantage — the thing that keeps a dividend growing for decades.
details: This is the lens you will use to judge competitive advantage forever.
Learn the five moat sources: intangible assets, switching costs, network effects, cost advantage, efficient scale.

resources.label: The Little Book That Builds Wealth — Pat Dorsey

tasks.title: Read “Common Stocks and Uncommon Profits”
tasks.summary: Fisher's qualitative 'scuttlebutt' method complements the numbers.
tasks.whatYouGain: The qualitative 'scuttlebutt' skill: judging a business by what customers, suppliers, and rivals reveal, not just its numbers.

resources.label: Common Stocks and Uncommon Profits — Philip Fisher

tasks.title: Begin “The Essays of Warren Buffett”
tasks.summary: Start the business-quality and owner-earnings sections — your ongoing scripture.
tasks.whatYouGain: Direct exposure to how the best capital allocator alive thinks about owning businesses and owner earnings.
details: This book is read over years, not a weekend. Begin the business-quality passages now.

resources.label: The Essays of Warren Buffett — ed. Lawrence Cunningham

tasks.title: Analyse 3 companies you use weekly
tasks.summary: How do they make money? Who is the customer? What's the moat? What could kill them?
tasks.whatYouGain: The ability to explain any business model in plain language — and to notice when you can't, which is itself a red flag.
details: Pick your phone maker, your bank, and a grocery brand.
Write four sentences each: business model, customer, moat, and the biggest 10-year threat.

tasks.title: Run a moat-rating drill
tasks.summary: Rate each company on all five moat types: none / narrow / wide.
tasks.whatYouGain: A repeatable way to grade a moat's type and width, turning a fuzzy gut feeling into a defensible rating.
details: Justify each rating in a single sentence. Apply the 'explain it in 3 jargon-free sentences' test.

tasks.title: Milestone: one-page business breakdown
tasks.summary: Pick one company: model, moat width, pricing power, risks, and the 20-year ownership test.
tasks.whatYouGain: Your first end-to-end business analysis, and a sharper sense of which companies you'd happily own for 20 years.
details: Ask Buffett's question: would you want to own this whole business for 20 years even if the market closed for a decade?
If the answer is no, you have learned something important about that company.

stages.title: Reading Financial Statements
stages.summary: The longest foundational stage. Everything later depends on reading the three statements fluently.
objectives: Read and link the income statement, balance sheet, and cash flow statement.
Compute and interpret ROE, ROIC, debt-to-equity, interest coverage, and margins.
Distinguish accounting profit from real cash — the difference that protects dividends.
caseStudies: Johnson & Johnson — a Dividend King; study its fortress balance sheet and how consistent free cash flow funds 60+ years of raises.
Microsoft — how the shift to cloud/subscriptions transformed the cash flow statement into a free-cash-flow machine.
mistakes: Trusting net income over cash flow — earnings can be massaged; sustained cash is hard to fake.
Looking at one year instead of the 5–10 year trend.
Being dazzled by high ROE that is really just high leverage — check ROIC and debt together.
Treating goodwill as real, liquid value.

tasks.title: Read “Financial Statements” by Thomas Ittelson
tasks.summary: Builds the three statements from a startup's first transactions — best book for a technical mind.
tasks.whatYouGain: Genuine fluency in the three financial statements — the literacy everything else in investing is built on.

resources.label: Financial Statements — Thomas Ittelson

tasks.title: Read “Warren Buffett and the Interpretation of Financial Statements”
tasks.summary: Which lines on the statements signal a durable competitive advantage.
tasks.whatYouGain: An eye for the specific statement lines that betray, or fake, a durable competitive advantage.

resources.label: Warren Buffett and the Interpretation of Financial Statements — Mary Buffett & David Clark

tasks.title: Pull a 10-K and build a 5-year free-cash-flow sheet
tasks.summary: From SEC EDGAR, compute FCF = Operating Cash Flow − Capital Expenditures yourself.
tasks.whatYouGain: The hands-on skill of pulling a real 10-K and computing free cash flow yourself, instead of trusting a website's number.
details: Read the Management's Discussion & Analysis (MD&A) first — the company explaining itself in plain English.
Tabulate 5 years of revenue, net income, operating cash flow, capex, and your computed FCF.

resources.label: SEC EDGAR — 10-K and 10-Q filings

tasks.title: Compute the full ratio set over 5 years
tasks.summary: ROE, ROIC, debt-to-equity, interest coverage, and three margins — trends matter more than any single year.
tasks.whatYouGain: A diagnostic toolkit of ratios — and the habit of reading 5-year trends rather than a single snapshot.
details: ROIC above the cost of capital, sustained, is the quantitative fingerprint of a moat.
Interest coverage below ~3x is a warning sign.

resources.label: Aswath Damodaran — free NYU lectures & spreadsheets

tasks.title: Run the earnings-quality test
tasks.summary: Plot net income vs operating cash flow over 5 years — do they move together?
tasks.whatYouGain: A fast lie-detector for earnings quality: whether reported profit is actually backed by cash.
details: If profit rises but cash does not, investigate before trusting the earnings.

tasks.title: Milestone: three-statement health check
tasks.summary: A 2–3 page write-up of 5-year trends, your ratios, your FCF, and a verdict on dividend capacity.
tasks.whatYouGain: Confidence to judge, on your own, whether a company truly generates the cash to sustain and grow its dividend.
details: End with one paragraph: is this company's cash generation capable of sustaining and growing a dividend?

stages.title: The Heart of Dividend Investing
stages.summary: The core of your specialisation: dividend metrics, safety, traps, sectors, taxes, and inflation.
objectives: Master yield, growth, payout ratio, and free-cash-flow coverage — and what each signals.
Assess dividend safety and spot dividend traps before they cut.
Understand how staples, utilities, REITs, and banks behave differently.
caseStudies: Realty Income — a REIT analysed on AFFO, not EPS; why a high payout ratio is structural, not dangerous.
PepsiCo — a Dividend King combining snacks and beverages; staples-sector dividend growth.
Unilever & Nestlé — international staples; currency effects, foreign withholding tax, and geographic resilience.
mistakes: Yield chasing — a collapsing price mechanically inflates the yield right before a cut.
Judging REITs and utilities by the wrong metric (use AFFO/FFO for REITs, not EPS).
Prioritising starting yield over dividend growth over a 30-year horizon.
Ignoring the free-cash-flow payout ratio, which is more honest than the earnings-based one.
Assuming a long streak guarantees safety — always re-underwrite the business.

tasks.title: Read “The Single Best Investment”
tasks.summary: The clearest articulation of the dividend-growth philosophy: quality + yield + growth.
tasks.whatYouGain: The core dividend-growth philosophy in one place: buy quality, at a fair yield, that grows — the spine of this whole roadmap.

resources.label: The Single Best Investment — Lowell Miller

tasks.title: Read “The Ultimate Dividend Playbook”
tasks.summary: A rigorous, practical framework for evaluating dividend safety and growth.
tasks.whatYouGain: A rigorous framework for separating safe, growing dividends from fragile ones.

resources.label: The Ultimate Dividend Playbook — Josh Peters
Berkshire Hathaway shareholder letters

tasks.title: Calculate yield & payout ratios for 5 companies
tasks.summary: Both earnings-based and FCF-based payout ratios across different sectors.
tasks.whatYouGain: The exact calculations behind yield and payout ratios, and an intuition for what 'normal' looks like in each sector.
details: Dividend Yield = Annual Dividend per Share ÷ Share Price.
Payout Ratio = Dividends ÷ Net Income; FCF Payout = Dividends ÷ Free Cash Flow.
Notice how a 'normal' payout ratio differs by sector.

tasks.title: Build a Dividend Tracker spreadsheet
tasks.summary: Yield, yield on cost, payout ratios, growth streak, 5-year dividend CAGR, projected income.
tasks.whatYouGain: A personal dividend tracker you'll reuse for life, turning scattered data into a clear income picture.
details: Columns: ticker, shares, cost basis, price, dividend/share, yield, yield on cost, payout ratio, FCF payout, growth streak, 5-yr dividend CAGR, projected annual income.
This becomes a tool you use for life.

resources.label: Simply Safe Dividends — educational articles

tasks.title: Analyse cash flow for dividend coverage
tasks.summary: Show 5 years of free cash flow vs dividends paid for one company.
tasks.whatYouGain: The single most honest test of dividend safety: whether free cash flow comfortably covers the payout.
details: Is the dividend comfortably covered by cash? Is coverage trending safer or riskier?

tasks.title: Run a spot-the-trap drill
tasks.summary: Find 3 stocks yielding 8%+ and diagnose why — without buying.
tasks.whatYouGain: Trap radar — the ability to look at a juicy 8% yield and diagnose the danger before it cuts.
details: Is the high yield caused by a falling price signalling distress, or a payout exceeding free cash flow?

tasks.title: Milestone: Dividend-Safety Scorecard
tasks.summary: Score 5 companies across 3+ sectors on five safety dimensions and rank them.
tasks.whatYouGain: A repeatable scorecard that ranks dividend safety across companies and sectors, so picks are evidence-based, not hopeful.
details: Score each 1–5, rank safest to riskiest, and justify the top and bottom names in a paragraph each.
checklist: FCF payout ratio comfortable for this type of business?
Balance sheet sound, with strong interest coverage?
Business model stable/recurring rather than cyclical?
Long, unbroken dividend-growth history?
Revenue and free cash flow growing, so the dividend can keep rising?

stages.title: Business Valuation
stages.summary: Know roughly whether you are paying a fair, full, or foolish price. Concepts over heavy math.
objectives: Interpret multiples: P/E, P/B, EV/EBITDA, PEG — and their limits.
Understand the logic of the Dividend Discount Model and Discounted Cash Flow.
Use a margin of safety as the bridge between value and price.
caseStudies: Coca-Cola — a wonderful business bought at a fair price and held for decades; 'fair' still has a ceiling.
Microsoft in 2000 — a great business at a ~60x P/E went nowhere for over a decade. Price always matters.
mistakes: False precision — a DCF output of '$147.32' implies confidence you do not have; think in ranges.
Garbage-in assumptions reverse-engineered to justify a desired answer.
Using the wrong multiple (P/B on a brand company; P/E on a cyclical at peak earnings).
Confusing a low multiple with cheapness — sometimes it correctly reflects a dying business.
Overpaying for quality 'because it's the best business'.

tasks.title: Read “The Five Rules for Successful Stock Investing”
tasks.summary: Ties business quality directly to valuation, without heavy math.
tasks.whatYouGain: An intuition for tying business quality to a sensible price, without drowning in spreadsheets.

resources.label: The Five Rules for Successful Stock Investing — Pat Dorsey

tasks.title: Read “The Little Book of Valuation”
tasks.summary: Damodaran makes DCF and multiples intuitive; pair with his free spreadsheets.
tasks.whatYouGain: Working command of both relative and intrinsic valuation, plus the free tools to do it yourself.

resources.label: The Little Book of Valuation — Aswath Damodaran

tasks.title: Compare multiples across 3 peers
tasks.summary: P/E, P/B (if relevant), EV/EBITDA, and yield vs each company's 10-year average.
tasks.whatYouGain: The skill of judging cheap vs expensive against a company's own history and its peers — context, not just a raw multiple.
details: Who is cheap, fair, or expensive relative to its own history — and why?

tasks.title: Build a simple Dividend Discount Model and stress-test it
tasks.summary: Value ≈ next dividend ÷ (required return − growth). Change growth by ±1% and watch it swing.
tasks.whatYouGain: A simple valuation model for stable growers, and a healthy humility about how much assumptions swing the answer.
details: The lesson is humility: small assumption changes move the answer a lot. Works only for stable growers.

tasks.title: Build a one-page Discounted Cash Flow
tasks.summary: Present value of future free cash flows; learn the engine, then distrust any tortured DCF.
tasks.whatYouGain: Understanding of the DCF engine from the inside — enough to use it, and enough to distrust a tortured one.

resources.label: Aswath Damodaran — valuation spreadsheets

tasks.title: Milestone: two-method fair-value estimate
tasks.summary: One relative valuation and one intrinsic valuation, giving a range and a margin-of-safety buy price.
tasks.whatYouGain: The ability to produce a fair-value range and a disciplined buy price you'll wait for instead of chasing.
details: End with: fairly valued / overvalued / undervalued — and the buy price you would wait for.

stages.title: Risk Management & Macroeconomics
stages.summary: Identify real risks (not short-term volatility), understand the macro forces that move dividend stocks, and build behavioural defences.
objectives: Separate permanent loss of capital from temporary volatility.
Understand how interest rates, inflation, and the cycle affect dividend stocks.
Defend against your own biases with written process and automation.
caseStudies: JPMorgan Chase — cyclical/financial risk: strong dividends in good times, real vulnerability in crises and to regulation.
A moat-erosion cautionary tale (e.g. Kodak or GE) — a famous name and long streak did not prevent permanent loss.
mistakes: Equating volatility with risk — a volatile durable business is safer than a stable-looking decaying one.
Becoming a macro forecaster and tinkering on rate predictions.
Underestimating your own behaviour in panics and manias.
Assuming 'blue chip' means 'safe forever'.

tasks.title: Read “The Most Important Thing”
tasks.summary: The deepest accessible book on risk, cycles, and second-level thinking.
tasks.whatYouGain: A deeper definition of risk — permanent loss, not volatility — and second-level thinking that sees what the crowd misses.

resources.label: The Most Important Thing — Howard Marks
Oaktree memos — Howard Marks

tasks.title: Read the key parts of “Thinking, Fast and Slow”
tasks.summary: The science of the cognitive biases that wreck investors.
tasks.whatYouGain: Names for the cognitive biases that quietly sabotage investors: the first step to defending against your own brain.

resources.label: Thinking, Fast and Slow — Daniel Kahneman

tasks.title: Run a risk-categorisation drill
tasks.summary: For one holding, list every way it could become a permanent loss vs a temporary drop.
tasks.whatYouGain: A clear separation between a temporary price drop and a real, permanent loss of capital — the only risk that matters long term.
details: Permanent loss is the only risk that matters long term. Separate it clearly from volatility.

tasks.title: Analyse interest-rate sensitivity
tasks.summary: Explain how rising rates would affect a utility, a REIT, a bank, and a staple — and why.
tasks.whatYouGain: An understanding of how interest rates move different dividend sectors, so rate news becomes legible instead of scary.
details: Interest rates are the gravity of all asset prices and raise borrowing costs for debt-heavy firms.

tasks.title: Do a behavioural self-audit
tasks.summary: Revisit your crash-reaction plan; be honest about how you felt in the last drawdown.
tasks.whatYouGain: An honest read on your own behaviour under stress, and stronger written rules to govern your next downturn.
details: Strengthen your written rules so process, not emotion, governs your next downturn.

tasks.title: Milestone: pre-mortem on a “safe” stock
tasks.summary: Imagine a beloved holding failed in 10 years and write what went wrong.
tasks.whatYouGain: Inoculation against overconfidence — practice arguing against your own favourite holdings before the market does.
details: Arguing against your own favourites is the best inoculation against overconfidence.

stages.title: Portfolio Construction
stages.summary: Assemble holdings into a resilient, diversified, income-growing portfolio with clear rules.
objectives: Diversify across sectors and geographies without diluting into mediocrity.
Set position-sizing, reinvestment (DRIP), rebalancing, and sell rules.
Balance current income against future dividend growth.
caseStudies: Apple — a modest dividend plus enormous buybacks; the textbook case for shareholder yield and capital allocation.
A full model portfolio — assemble the case-study companies and find the sector and geographic gaps.
mistakes: Over-diversifying into a closet index of names you cannot follow, or under-diversifying into correlated bets.
Letting one sector dominate (e.g. all high-yield REITs and utilities).
Reaching for yield at the portfolio level, dragging up risk.
Selling for the wrong reasons — reacting to price, not to the business.
Overtrading and over-rebalancing, incurring taxes and interrupting compounding.

tasks.title: Revisit the portfolio chapters of “The Single Best Investment”
tasks.summary: Portfolio-level guidance for building a dividend-growth machine.
tasks.whatYouGain: Portfolio-level thinking: how individual good picks combine into a resilient income machine.

resources.label: The Single Best Investment — Lowell Miller

tasks.title: Study the Bogleheads wiki
tasks.summary: Timeless, jargon-free material on allocation, account location, and behaviour.
tasks.whatYouGain: Timeless, jargon-free principles on allocation, account placement, and staying the course.

resources.label: Bogleheads wiki

tasks.title: Run a sector-mapping drill
tasks.summary: Map 15–20 quality dividend payers to sectors; find overconcentration and gaps.
tasks.whatYouGain: A map of your sector exposure that reveals hidden overconcentration and gaps before they hurt you.
details: Deliberately spread across staples, healthcare, utilities, financials, tech, and REITs.

tasks.title: Design a position-sizing model
tasks.summary: Target weights with a per-stock cap (~5–8%) and a per-sector cap.
tasks.whatYouGain: Sensible rules for how big each position and sector can grow — diversification without diluting into mediocrity.
details: Compute the projected blended yield and weighted dividend-growth rate.

tasks.title: Project a reinvestment scenario
tasks.summary: Model 25 years with dividends reinvested and growing vs not reinvesting.
tasks.whatYouGain: Hard numbers on the reinvestment flywheel, quantifying exactly what reinvested dividends add over decades.
details: Quantify the gap. The dividends-buy-shares-that-pay-dividends flywheel is where wealth is built.

tasks.title: Write your sell rules
tasks.summary: A numbered list you would follow under stress.
tasks.whatYouGain: A pre-committed sell checklist that lets process, not panic, decide when you exit.
checklist: Sell when the original thesis breaks (moat erosion, deteriorating financials).
Sell when the dividend is cut or its safety collapses.
Sell when wildly overvalued with clearly better uses for the capital.
Do NOT sell because the price dropped and nothing else changed.
Do NOT sell out of boredom, a scary headline, or a hot tip elsewhere.

tasks.title: Milestone: build a model dividend-growth portfolio
tasks.summary: 15–25 holdings with weights, caps, projected yield and growth, plus reinvestment and rebalancing policy.
tasks.whatYouGain: A complete model portfolio — a full dress rehearsal for managing real money with real rules.
details: Attach your sell-rule checklist. This is a dry run of the real thing.

stages.title: Independent Analysis & Your Framework
stages.summary: Integrate everything into one repeatable process, then produce a decision-grade report on your own.
objectives: Run a single, repeatable workflow on any stock from scratch.
Produce a complete investment report independently.
Codify your personal investment philosophy.
mistakes: Skipping the primary sources — read the 10-K and the shareholder letter yourself.
Reaching a decision without writing down the single most important reason for it.
Treating the framework as fixed — revisit and refine it every year.

tasks.title: Revisit “The Essays of Warren Buffett”
tasks.summary: Read the capital-allocation passages again — they land differently now.
tasks.whatYouGain: A second, wiser pass through Buffett's capital-allocation thinking, which reads differently now that you have the tools.

resources.label: The Essays of Warren Buffett — ed. Lawrence Cunningham

tasks.title: Read a full 10-K and shareholder letter
tasks.summary: The habit that separates independent investors from tip-followers.
tasks.whatYouGain: The defining habit of an independent investor: reading primary sources yourself instead of following tips.
details: Do this for every company you seriously consider. Take notes on moat, capital allocation, and risks.

tasks.title: Internalise the independent stock-analysis checklist
tasks.summary: Use this for every stock, every time. A 'no' or 'I can't tell' is itself an answer.
tasks.whatYouGain: A single repeatable checklist you can run on any stock, forever — your personal analytical engine.
checklist: Business quality: can I explain how it makes money in 3 plain sentences, within my competence?
Moat: what type and width, and is it widening or eroding? Pricing power?
Management: sensible capital allocation, dividend treated as a commitment, candid with owners?
Financial health: 5–10 yr trends in revenue, margins, ROIC, FCF; manageable debt; good earnings quality?
Dividend sustainability: comfortable FCF payout (AFFO for REITs); growth record; no trap signals?
Valuation: multiples vs history and peers; intrinsic estimate as a range; margin of safety today?
Risks: top 3 paths to permanent loss; rate and cycle sensitivity; disruption risk?
Growth prospects: where does future growth come from, and how long is the runway?
Fair value estimate: conservative range, and my buy price with margin of safety.
Decision: Buy, Hold, or Avoid — and the single most important reason.

tasks.title: Write your Personal Investment Framework
tasks.summary: One to two pages: philosophy, what you buy, what you pay, sizing, sell rules, behaviour, routine.
tasks.whatYouGain: Your written investment framework: the difference between owning a few dividend stocks and having a process that compounds for 40 years.
details: This is the real final deliverable — the difference between owning some dividend stocks and having a process that compounds for 40 years.

tasks.title: Capstone: three-stock investment report
tasks.summary: Analyse three real dividend stocks from three sectors and justify Buy / Hold / Avoid for each.
tasks.whatYouGain: Proof you can do it alone — three full, decision-grade analyses and the confidence that you no longer need anyone's stock tips.
details: Three companies, three distinct sectors (e.g. a staple, a REIT or utility, a financial or tech grower).
Work from primary sources: read each latest 10-K and shareholder letter; pull the data yourself.

For each, a 2–4 page report running the full checklist: business & moat, 5–10 yr financials, dividend safety, two-method valuation with a buy price, top-3 risks and a pre-mortem, and a justified Buy/Hold/Avoid.
Finish with a half-page portfolio note: how the three would fit together, and which you would act on first.
